title: Gołąbki
subtitle: Polish stuffed cabbage rolls in tomato sauce
excerpt: Parcels of pork mince and rice wrapped in blanched cabbage leaves and braised in a tomato and stock sauce until tender. A Sunday dinner dish across Poland, made in large batches and better on the second day.
sourceNotes: Gołąbki (little doves) are one of the most widely made dishes in Polish home cooking, with regional variations across every part of the country. The tomato sauce is the most common finish; some regions use a mushroom sauce or plain stock. The rice-and-pork filling with marjoram is the standard. Made in quantities of twenty or more for family gatherings, gołąbki are quintessentially batch-cook food.

content.text: Gołąbki take time, not skill. The main task is blanching the cabbage leaves until pliable enough to roll without splitting, then assembling the parcels neatly so they hold together during the long braise. The rice goes into the filling raw: it cooks inside the roll, absorbing the meat juices and stock, and this is what gives gołąbki their characteristic texture.

Use a whole cabbage and remove leaves one by one from the core end as they soften in boiling water. Large, undamaged leaves are what you want. Small leaves or broken ones can be used to line the bottom of the casserole dish, where they protect the rolls from scorching.

Bring a large pot of salted water to the boil. Cut a deep X into the base of the {{cabbage-white}} and lower it into the water. As the outer leaves soften (2 to 3 minutes), peel them away with tongs and set aside on a tray. Continue until you have 12 to 14 large, pliable leaves. Pat dry. Trim any thick central rib on each leaf with a small knife so the leaf lies flat.

Mix the {{pork-mince}}, {{long-grain-rice}}, {{onion}}, {{eggs}}, {{marjoram}}, {{sea-salt-fine}}, and {{black-pepper-ground}} together until combined. Place a heaped tablespoon of filling near the base of a cabbage leaf. Fold the sides in, then roll tightly from the base upward to form a firm parcel. Repeat with the remaining leaves and filling.

Heat the oven to 180°C. Melt the {{unsalted-butter}} in a saucepan over medium heat. Add the {{tomato-puree}} and cook for 1 minute. Add the blended {{tinned-tomatoes}} and {{stock-chicken}}, stir, and season. Line the base of a casserole dish with any remaining cabbage leaves. Arrange the rolls seam-side down in a single layer. Pour the sauce over. Cover tightly with a lid or foil and bake for 80 to 90 minutes until the rice is cooked through and the rolls are tender.

items.fix: Place rolls seam-side down so the weight of the sauce holds them closed. If they continue to unravel, secure each with a toothpick and remove before serving
items.cause: Not rolled tightly enough, or seam placed upward

items.fix: Add more hot stock (100 ml), recover tightly, and bake for a further 20 minutes. The rice needs both moisture and heat to cook through
items.cause: Too little sauce, or oven too low

items.fix: The filling should be firmly packed into each leaf with no air pockets. The rice expands as it cooks, so measure carefully
items.cause: Filling too loosely packed or too much rice

content.text: Gołąbki appear at Sunday dinners and at family gatherings where feeding a crowd is the task. They are made in large batches, often by multiple generations working together: one person making the filling, another blanching the leaves, a third rolling and packing the casserole dish. The name (little doves) refers to the shape of the finished rolls. The dish is also made at Christmas and Easter in many families, alongside the other meat dishes that anchor those tables.
